coredata.dc:title: Erbium-doped graphene quantum dots with up- and down-conversion luminescence for effective detection of ferric ions in water and human serum
coredata.dc:creator: Huynh, Trung Viet
Anh, Nguyen Thi Ngoc
Darmanto, Win
Doong, Ruey-An
coredata.dc:description: Herein, the erbium-doped graphene quantum dots (Er-GQDs) with up- and down-conversion fluorescence have, for the first time, been hydrothermally fabricated at lactose-to-erbium pentahydrate ratio of 50, and then employed as the nanosensing probe to detect Fe3+ ions in aqueous and human serum samples. The surface characterization shows that the 1.8 at% Er is successful incorporated into 2–8 nm GQD lattice to form Er-GQD nanoparticles. The Er3+-doped GQDs exhibit both up and down-conversion luminescence properties after excitation with 730-nm visible light and 360-nm UV light, respectively. Both up- and down-conversion fluorescence properties of Er-GQDs show excellent analytical performance toward Fe3+ detection. A wide dynamic range of 4 orders of magnitude with limits of detection of 2.8–11.2 nM is observed when down-conversion property of Er-GQD is used for Fe3+ detection in PBS and human serum. Moreover, both up- and down-conversion fluorescence behaviors exhibit superior selectivity over 16 metal ion and 19 organic interferences. Results clearly indicate the feasibility of using Er-GQDs with both up- and down-conversion fluorescence properties as the nanosensing probe for highly selective and sensitive detection of metal ions in various environmental media, which can extend the application of up-conversion application from inorganic materials to carbon based nanomaterials for label-free detection of analysts in environmental and biological samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Erbium-doped graphene quantum dots (Er-GQDs)
Lanthanide
Up-conversion
Down-conversion
Ferric ions
Selectivity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of ferric ions in aqueous solutions by Er-GQDs
Detection of Fe3+ in human serum
pH effect on fluorescence property of Er-GQDs
Detection of Fe3+ by Er-GQDs in human serum
Selectivity of Er-GQDs toward Fe3+ detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images and particle size distribution (inset), (b) HRTEM image, (c) fast Fourier transform (FFT), (d) AFM images, and (e) EDS spectrum of as-prepared Er-GQDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum and deconvoluted (b) C 1s (c) O 1s, and (d) Er 4d peak, and (e) FTIR spectra of Er-doped GQDs, and (f) time–resolved fluorescence spectra of pure GQDs and Er–GQDs.
(a) The Raman spectra and (b) XRD patterns of as-prepared GQDs and Er-GQDs.

(a) UV–vis spectra and photo image (inset) of Er-GQDs and GQDs, (b) down-conversion based fluorescence spectra of Er-GQDs at excitation wavelengths of 310 – 420 nm, and (c) up-conversion fluorescence spectra and possible mechanism irradiated with visible light at 680 – 730 nm.

(a) Influence of pH value on the fluorescence intensity of Er-GQDs at pHs 5 – 10, and photo images of Er-GQDs in the (b) absence and (c) presence of 400 μM Fe3+ at pHs 5 – 10 under UV light irradiation. Error bars in the figure were the standard deviation of triplicate analyses.

(a) Effect of Fe3+ concentration on the down-conversion fluorescence spectra of Er-GQDs, (b) change in fluorescence intensity as a function of Fe3+ concentration ranging from 0 – 400 μM, (c) Up-conversion fluorescence spectra of Er-GQDs in the presence of 0 – 400 μM Fe3+ ions, and (d) linear relationship between up-conversion fluorescence intensity and Fe3+ concentration. The excitation wavelengths for down- and up-conversions are 360 and 730 nm, respectively. Error bars in the figure were the standard deviations of triplicate analyses.

(a) Effect of Fe3+ concentration on the down-conversion fluorescence spectra of Er-GQDs in human serum, (b) change in fluorescence intensity as a function of Fe3+ concentration in human serum, (c) Up-conversion fluorescence spectra of Er-GQDs in human serum in the presence of 0 – 400 μM Fe3+ ions, and (d) linear relationship between up-conversion fluorescence intensity and Fe3+ concentration in human serum. The excitation wavelengths for down- and up-conversions are 360 and 730 nm, respectively. Error bars in the figure were the standard deviation of triplicate analyses.

Influence of various 16 metal ion interferences on the selectivity of Fe3+ by Er – GQDs using (a) down-conversion and (b) up-conversion fluorescence properties. Error bars in the figure were the standard deviation of triplicate analyses.
MTT assays of MCF-7 cells in the presence of 0 – 1000 μg mL−1 Er-GQDs. Error bars in the figure were the standard deviation of triplicate analyses.
Illustration of fabrication of Er-based graphene quantum dots (Er-GQDs) for up- and down-conversion detection of ferric ions in aqueous solution and human serum samples.
Comparison of the analytical performance of various sensing materials for the detection Fe3+ ions.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: GC-AuNP: Glycol chitosan-Au nanoparticles; BIODIPY: boron-dipyrromethene; CD: carbon dots; DA-GQD: dopamine-graphene quantum dots; CQD: carbon quantum dots.

head.ce:title: Erbium-doped graphene quantum dots with up- and down-conversion luminescence for effective detection of ferric ions in water and human serum

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Analytical and Environmental Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, the erbium-doped graphene quantum dots (Er-GQDs) with up- and down-conversion fluorescence have, for the first time, been hydrothermally fabricated at lactose-to-erbium pentahydrate ratio of 50, and then employed as the nanosensing probe to detect Fe3+ ions in aqueous and human serum samples. The surface characterization shows that the 1.8 at% Er is successful incorporated into 2–8 nm GQD lattice to form Er-GQD nanoparticles. The Er3+-doped GQDs exhibit both up and down-conversion luminescence properties after excitation with 730-nm visible light and 360-nm UV light, respectively. Both up- and down-conversion fluorescence properties of Er-GQDs show excellent analytical performance toward Fe3+ detection. A wide dynamic range of 4 orders of magnitude with limits of detection of 2.8–11.2 nM is observed when down-conversion property of Er-GQD is used for Fe3+ detection in PBS and human serum. Moreover, both up- and down-conversion fluorescence behaviors exhibit superior selectivity over 16 metal ion and 19 organic interferences. Results clearly indicate the feasibility of using Er-GQDs with both up- and down-conversion fluorescence properties as the nanosensing probe for highly selective and sensitive detection of metal ions in various environmental media, which can extend the application of up-conversion application from inorganic materials to carbon based nanomaterials for label-free detection of analysts in environmental and biological samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have recently been regarded as a new graphene based nanomaterials for nanosensing, energy conversion and storage because of their low cost, low toxicity, simple fabrication and superior optoelectrochemical property in comparison with other materials [1–3]. Moreover, GQDs based nanomaterial is a promising label-free fluorescent probe for the effective and real-time monitoring of toxic chemicals [4,5]. Since the quantum yield of pure GQDs is relatively low, several heteroatoms such as N, S, and B have been used as the effective dopants to enhance the quantum yield of GQDs, which can sensitively and selectively detect metal ions [6–8], organic pollutions [1], and biomarkers [2,9], Traditionally, UV light is needed to irradiate GQDs and then use the down-conversion luminescence to detect analysts [7]. The up-conversion of nanoparticles such as NaYF4:Yb/Er and NaGdF4:Yb/Tm have also been used for biomedical application such as bio-imaging and drug delivery [10]. However, the development of up-conversion based organic nanomaterials for nano-sensing has received less attention.

The rare-earth elements such as erbium (Er), europium (Eu), gadolinium (Gd), and ytterbium (Yb) have recently been reported to be one of the effective dopants to enhance the up-conversion efficiency of inorganic nanoparticles for biomedical applications [11]. The rare-earth doped materials could release the up-conversion photoluminescence to expand the practical application in optical sensing, bio-sensing, and MRI image [12]. Compared to the up-conversion luminescence detection by inorganic metal oxides, however, only limited studies have combined the lanthanide group dopants with organic nanomaterials for the detection of analysts [13,14]. Wu and Yan [13] have doped Eu(III) into metal organic framework (MOF) for optical sensing of organic compounds such as chloroform, acetone, and pyridine. Among lanthanide groups used, erbium ions (Er) is considered as one of the most suitable dopants to enhance the up-conversion fluorescence property of organic nanomaterials because of their slowly tarnishable behaviors in the air than the other rare-earth ions such as Yb, Eu and Tb [15].

Ferric iron (Fe3+) is an relevantly important metal ion involving many physiological and pathological processes in human body [16]. Both low and high concentrations of Fe3+ in human body can cause different diseases related to anemia, cancers, and organ dysfunction [17]. Therefore, the quick and precise detection of ferric ions in both water and biological samples is important. Nowadays, several techniques for the ferric ion detection in the aqueous and human serum samples have been developed [18,19]. In addition, some fluorescence-based nanosensing techniques using GQDs as the down-conversion fluorescent dye to detect a wide variety of metal ions and pollutants have been reported [7,20,21]. Although these GQD-based nanomaterials exhibit low limit of detection (LOD) and good selectivity, the high energy-needed UV light is essential to excite the GQDs to produce down-conversion fluorescence for detection. In additional, the down-conversion materials, which use short-wavelength excitation light, are well-known materials with strong scattering as well as inherent weaknesses of penetration depth in biological tissues [11]. Up to now, the development of up-conversion materials for the effective determination of environmental pollutants has received less attention. Moreover, the fabrication of rare-earth doped GQDs with both up- and down-conversion luminescence properties for the effective determination of metal ions have not been reported yet.

In this study, we have first synthesized the Er-doped GQDs by one-pot hydrothermal methods as the label-free fluorescent probe for the up- and down-conversion detection of Fe3+ in water and biological samples. As shown in Scheme 1 , the GQDs prepared by lactose are doped with Er ions to form Er-GQDs nanomaterials. The Er-GQDs exhibit excellent down- and up-conversion luminescence properties after irradiation with both 360-nm UV and 730-nm visible lights, respectively. The developed Er-GQDs also show the highly sensitive and selective detection of nano-molar concentrations of Fe3+ in water and human serum samples when both up- and down-conversion fluorescence are employed. The Er-GQDs show an excellent response to Fe3+ in the wide range of 0.01–140 μM with LODs of 2.8 nM in PBS and 28 nM in human serum. The selectivity of Fe3+ detection over 13 metal ion interferences is examined. Moreover, the cytotoxicity of Er-GQDs to MCF-7 is evaluated to understand the feasibility of using Er-GQDs as the promising nanosensing probe for environmental and biomedical applications.

Lactose, erbium nitrate pentahydrate (Er(NO3)3·5H2O) and iron chloride (FeCl3·6H2O) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Human serum was a product of Gemini Bio Co. The dialysis bag with 1000-Da MWCO was obtained from Membrane Filtration Products, Inc. All the chemicals were used as received without further treatment. In addition, distilled deionized water (DDI water, 18.2 MΩ cm) was used throughout the experimental course.

The Er-GQDs were hydrothermally fabricated at 200 °C for 4 h with lactose and erbium nitrate pentahydrate as the carbon source and dopant, respectively. In brief, 500 mg of lactose and 11 mg of erbium salt were mixed with 5 mL of DDI water and then ultra-sonicated for 5 min. The well-dispersed mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless tube and then heated to 200 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the Er-GQD containing mixture was first filtrated with 0.22 μm PVDF membrane to remove solids, and then dialyzed the supernatant with a 1000-Da dialysis bag for 8 h in the presence of DDI water to remove those unreacted substances.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-ARM 200 F) and high resolution TEM (HR-TEM, JEM-2010) from JEOL Co. (Tokyo, Japan) were used to identify the surface morphology and particle size of Er-GQDs. The topographical information and thickness of Er-GQDs were obtained with a scanning probe microscope (Agilent 5500) in the tapping mode. The crystallinity of Er-GQDs was examined with a Bruker D8 X-ray diffractometer (XRD). Moreover, an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Ulvac-PHI 1600) from Physical Electronics was employed to characterize the chemical species of Er-GQD based nanomaterials. The up- and down-conversion fluorescence spectra of Er-GQDs were recorded using Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer in the excitation wavelength range of 310–420 and 680–730 nm, respectively. The surface functional groups of Er-GQDs was identified with Horiba FT720 FTIR spectrophotometer. Moreover, Raman spectra of Er-GQDs were recorded by micro-Raman spectrometer (Burker Senterra).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of ferric ions in aqueous solutions by Er-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical performance of Er-GQDs was examined by using Fe3+ as the model ions. The stock solution of Fe3+ was prepared by dissolving 486.63 mg FeCl3 into 10 mL of DDI water. For Fe3+ detection, the working solution was prepared by adding 20 μL of Er-GQDs in 1 mL of phosphate buffered saline (PBS) at pH 7.5 ± 0.1, and then the stock Fe3+ solutions were added with different volumes to get the final Fe3+ concentrations of 0.01–400 μM. After 2 min of incubation, the fluorescence intensity of working solution was recorded at excitation wavelength of 360 nm for down-conversion and 730 nm for up-conversion. All the experiments were replicated for at least three times for quality control. The effect of pH on the analytical performance of Er-GQDs was investigated in the presence of 400 μM Fe3+ at pHs 5–10. Moreover, the selectivity of Fe3+ ions by Er-GQDs over other 13 metal ion interferences was investigated at pH 7.5. The concentration used for selectivity experiment was all 400 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: The working human serum solution was obtained via a 10-time dilution with 0.1 M PBS buffer as the diluent at pH 7.5. The nanosensing performance of Er-GQDs was analyzed under standard addition method to determine Fe3+ concentration in the spiked human serum. Moreover, the human serum studies were performed according to the guidelines and regulations of the ethics committee and the IRB of National Tsing Hua University (IRB No. 10802HM004).

The cytotoxicity of Er-GQDs was tested using 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay. The MCF-7 cells were incubated in a 48-well plate containing 5000 cells, and then 0–1,000 μg mL−1 Er-GQDs were added to the culture media and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C in the presence of 5% CO2. After incubation, the 48-well plate was washed with 0.1 M PBS at pH 7.5. The cell viability of MCF-7 in each well was determined by the absorbance using microplate reader at 570 and 600 nm.

The size distribution as well as morphology of the as-prepared Er-GQDs was first characterized. As illustrated in Fig. 1 a, the as-prepared Er-GQDs are homogenously dispersed with uniform lateral size. The particle sizes are in the range of 2–8 nm with average diameter around 4.7 ± 0.9 nm, which is corroborated with the reported results of doped GQDs [2,7]. Obviously, the HRTEM image clearly shows that the fringe of carbon lattice spacing distances of as-prepared Er-GQDs is 2.1 Å, which can be assigned as the (100) plane of graphene (Fig. 1b and c). Additional, the height in the AFM image is the range of 0.8–3.5 nm with an average of 1.0 nm, evidenced that the Er-GQDs only contain 2–9 layers in thickness of graphene structure (Fig. 1d). Moreover, the EDS spectrum as-prepared of Er-GQDs in Fig. 1e shows that the C, O and Er in the as-prepared Er-GQDs are 73.0, 25.2 and 1.8 at%, confirming the successful fabrication of doped GQDs using Er element as the effective dopant. It is noteworthy the GQDs are one kind of carbon quantum dots (CDs). Usually the thickness of GQDs (< 10 layers) is lower than that of CDs [21], and contains more graphene-like structures with sp2 hybridization. In this study, the AFM and HRTEM images indicate that 2–9 layers of carbon based materials include 0.21 nm of (100) graphene plane, which is shorter than 0.334 nm of (002) graphite plane. In addition, the combination of zigzag and armchair edges in HRTEM images is an evidence on the formation of GQDs [23] (Fig. S1, Supplementary data). These results signify that the as-prepared carbon-based nanomaterials may contain both CDs and GQDs, and GQDs are the predominant species in this study.

Fig. 2 a shows the XPS survey scan of Er-GQD nanomaterials. Two major peaks at 284 and 532 eV with one small peak at 168 eV are clearly observed. These peaks are the typical C 1s, O 1s and Er 3d signals, which is in good agreement with the EDS result. The deconvoluted XP C 1s spectrum of Er-GQDs exhibits four major peaks at 283.9, 286.0, 287.1, and 288.4 eV, corresponding to the CO, CO and OCO/COOH groups, respectively (Fig. 2b). Another two peaks at 284.8 and 285.5 eV is mainly from the aliphatic/aromatic (sp3/sp2) carbons with spectral component of CC and C–H bonds, which is attributed to the original GQDs [22,23]. Moreover, peaks at 530.3 and 530.9 eV in the deconvoluted O 1s peak can be assigned as the metal oxides (Fig. 2c), which is the linkage of Er with O elements [24]. The peak at 532.5 eV is typically the carboxylic CO peak. The OH group from either water molecule or carboxylic acid is observed at 531.7 eV. In addition, the XPS Er 4d peak after peak deconvolution exhibits the binding energy at 168.2 eV and 170 eV (Fig. 2d), which is the characteristic 4d5/2 and 4d3/2 of Er2O3 peaks, respectively [25,26].

The functional groups of Er-GQDs was further identified by FTIR. As shown in Fig. 2e, the as-prepared GQDs exhibit the characteristic OH band centered at 3440 cm−1, which means the GQD based nanomaterials is hydrophilic. Peaks at 1085, 1384, 1472, and 1634 cm-1 are CO, COH, CC, and CO functional groups, respectively. After doping Er to the as-prepared GQDs, the FTIR spectrum of Er-GQDs still shows those similar functional group peaks, but the bands of CO, COH, CC, which belong to the stretching and bending vibration of carboxylic groups, in the range of 1085 – 1472 cm−1 are red-shifted. Additionally, a footprint peak at 520 cm−1 is the vibration Er-O bond, indicating that Er has successfully coordinated with carboxylate group in the as-prepared GQDs to form the Er-GQDs nanosensing [27]. Moreover, the time-resolved fluorescence spectra of pure GQDs and Er-GQDs were further determined. As displayed in Fig. 2f, the fluorescence decay of pure GQDs is 7.3 ns, and then increases to 7.4 ns when erbium element is doped into pure GQDs. This result reflects the fact that the doping of GQDs with Er can prolong the fluorescence decay, the photon can be transferred from the triplet state of Er to the ground state of GQDs, resulting in the extension of the fluorescence decay of GQDs.

The atomic structure of carbon-based material has been characterized via Raman spectroscopy [28]. The Raman spectrum of GQDs in Fig. 3 a shows two representative peaks located at 1378 and 1587 cm−1, which are the D and G bands from the defect sites in the graphite and the tangential CC bond vibration, respectively [29]. After doping Er element onto the surface of GQDs, the peaks of D and G bands blue-shift to 1350 and 1568 cm−1, respectively, and the ID/IG ratio decreases slightly from 0.87 for the as-prepared GQDs to 0.86 for Er-GQDs. Moreover, the crystallinity of GQDs and Er–GQDs are examined by XRD. As shown in Fig. 3b, the XRD patterns display a broad peak centered at 25.6° 2θ, which can be evidenced as the (002) plane of graphite [30]. The (002) peak of Er-GQDs has shifted slightly to 26.2° 2θ after doping, indicating that the graphite structure of GQDs changes after doping with Er.

The optical property of Er-GQDs was examined by the irradiation of UV and visible lights. As illustrated in Fig. S2 (Supplementary data), the color of Er-GQDs is dopant-concentration dependent when the Er concentration increases from 1 to 22 mM. Under the irradiation of visible light, the color changes slightly from transparent in PBS to light yellow in Er-GQDs when Er is added to GQDs. In contrast, the Er-GQDs emit a strong blue fluorescence after the irradiation with 360-nm UV light, and the intensity increases as the Er concentration increases from 1 to 10 mM, and then maintains the similar fluorescence intensity at 15–22 mM. Interestingly, the color of blank control of PBS solution remains unchanged, which evidences that Er-GQDs can emit strongly bright blue fluorescence light for sensing purposes. Since Er is a rare-earth element and the fluorescence intensity has no obvious difference at 10–22 mM, the Er concentration of 10 mM is then selected for experiments.

Fig. 4 a shows the UV–vis spectra of pure GQDs and Er-GQDs. The as-prepared GQDs shows two peaks located at 260 and 330 nm. The peak at 260 nm is the characteristic peak of π-π* transition of CC bond [31], while another absorbance peak at 330 nm is attributed to the n-π* transition of CO bond of GQDs. After doping with Er onto the surface of GQDs, a blue-shift in CO bond clearly appears and the optical density of Er-GQDs decreases from 0.45 to 0.17. This means the formation of the new complex in the ground state, presumably due to the coordination of Er atoms to oxygen on the surface of GQDs to form Er-O backbone through Lewis acid-base coordination [32]. Moreover, the decrease in fluorescence intensity indicates that the Er-GQDs needs more energy to reach the excite state as well as to stabilize the molecules in Er-GQDs due to the quantum size effect [33].

The down-conversion fluorescence spectra of Er–GQDs was recorded under the excitation of wavelengths ranging from 310 to 420 nm. As shown in Fig. 4b, the fluorescence spectra of Er – GQDs exhibit emission peaks at 427–497 nm when the irradiation wavelength increases from 310 to 420 nm. Specifically, the fluorescence intensity increases with the slight peak shift from 428 to 440 nm when the wavelength increases from 310 to 360 nm. The peaks red-shift to a longer wavelength region of 440–497 nm with the decreased peak intensity when the excitation wavelength changes to 360–420 nm. It is also noteworthy that the width of the emission peaks in the study is wider than some recently reported results of doped GQDs [1,2,7,9], presumably attributed to the different dopants and quantum yields used for sensing purposes. The down-conversion efficiency of GQD-based nanomaterials is usually dependent on the quantum yield (QY) [34]. When using quinine sulfate as the standard solution for QY calculation, the QY of Er-GQDs is calculated to be 0.18, which is almost 2 times higher than that of as-prepared GQDs (0.096). However, the QY of Er-GQDs is lower than those GQDs doped with other elements such as N and S [1,2,7]. Since the emission of down-conversion fluorescence of Er-GQDs follows the intramolecular energy transfer mechanism, GQDs will absorb energy during the short-wavelength excitation, and results in the excitation to singlet state first and then to triplet state of oxygen based on Forster theory for intramolecular GQDs to slightly widen the spectra [35,36]. The electrons will transfer from GQDs to Er3+ according to the Dexter’s theory. It is likely that the excited GQD molecules transfer their energy to the erbium ions, depicting that Er–GQDs has a superior down-conversion luminescence property for nanosensing of analytes at 360 nm.

The up-conversion property Er-GQDs was also examined via by the irradiation with visible light. As shown in Fig. 4c, the excitation of Er-GQDs with visible light of 680–730 nm can produce emission peaks centered at 437–442 nm. The excitation of 730 nm can produce high intensity of up-conversion fluorescence at 442 nm. Several studies have investigated the up-conversion property of inorganic materials like NaYF4 in the presence of lanthanide elements, and found the up-conversion can be explained via triplet-triplet annihilation (TTA), which can be defined as a photo-physical phenomenon where the energy of two photons can be combined into a single photon with higher energy [37,38]. In this study, we found that the Er doped GQDs also exhibit TTA photon up-conversion behavior, which can extend the knowledge from inorganic system to organic materials. After the long-wavelength excitation, GQDs can serve as a sensitizer to cross-transfer energy from triplet state of GQDs to the triplet state of Er element, which serves as an acceptor, and subsequently leads to the regeneration of long-wavelength light at ground state of sensitizer when the photon transfers from the triplet state of GQDs to the triplet state of Er, it forms the long live triplet acceptor to support for one TTA event. In addition, the quantum yield (QY) and quantum efficiency (QE) were used to examine the down-conversion and up-conversion fluorescence of Er-GQDs, respectively. When using quinine sulfate as the standard solution for QY calculation, the QY of Er-GQDs is 0.18, which is almost 2 times higher than that of as-prepared GQDs (0.096) and previously reported study doped with different heteroatoms [24]. The QE is double of QY [11], which means that the QE of Er-GQDs is 0.36.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH effect on fluorescence property of Er-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: pH is one of the critical factors to influence the fluorescence intensity of sensing materials. Fig. 5 a shows the influence of pH on the down-conversion luminescence property of Er-GQDs in the presence and absence of Fe3+ at pHs 5–10. The fluorescence intensity of Er-GQDs without the addition of Fe3+ ions increases linearly from pH 5 to 7, and then decreases obviously under alkaline conditions of pH 8–10, which is consistent with the previously reported results using GQD based nanomaterials as the fluorescent dye [39]. The addition of 400 μM Fe3+ deceases the fluorescence intensity significantly. The photo images illustrate that the color of pure GQDs is quite bright blue at pHs 5–10 (Fig. 5b). However, the fluorescence fades obviously in the presence of Fe3+ ions, and the color is nearly diminished at pHs 8–10 (Fig. 5c). This result clearly indicates that pH 7–8 is optimal for the detection of Fe3+ by Er-GQDs. Since the pH value of human serum is around 7.4, the pH of 7.5 is therefore chosen for nanosensing purposes.

The analytical performance of Er-QGDs using down- and up-conversion fluorescence properties to detect Fe3+ ions was first evaluated in PBS at pH 7.5. Fig. 6 a illustrates the down-conversion fluorescence of Er–GQDs under 360-nm UV light irradiation in the presence of 0–400 μM Fe3+ ions. It is clear that the down-conversion fluorescence peak occurs at 440 nm, and the peak intensity decreases proportionally as the Fe3+ concentration increased from 0 to 400 μM. Moreover, a two-stage linear relationship, i.e. a linear calibration curve in the low concentration range of 10 nM – 1 μM and then follows by a wide dynamic range of 1–120 μM with both coefficient (R2) of 0.99, is clearly observed (Fig. 6b). The limit of detection (LOD) of Er-GQDs, determined by 3σL where σ is the relative standard deviation for 7 replicates, and L is the lowest Fe3+ concentration added, is 2.8 nM. Several studies have used the doped or surface modified GQDs including N-GQDs, S-GQDs and dopamine-modified GQDs for effective detection of Fe3+ ions in aqueous media, and the linear range and LOD were in the range of 2–3 orders of magnitude and 7.6–90 nM, respectively [21]. These results clearly signify the flexibility and superiority of Er-doped GQDs for down-conversion fluorescence detection of Fe3+ in PBS.

Several phenomena including static quenching, dynamic quenching, and inner filter effect can be used to explain the possible fluorescence quenching mechanism for GQD sensing [40]. To clarify the possible down-conversion detection mechanism of Fe3+ by Er-GQDs, the fluorescence life-time measurement of Er-GQDs in the absence and presence of 400 μM Fe3+ ions was performed using time-correlated single-photon counting technique. As shown in Fig. S3 (Supplementary data), the fluorescence decay of pure Er-GQDs in the time-resolved fluorescence spectra is 7.4 ns. The fluorescence emission is found at a time scale of 7.4 ns when 400 μM Fe3+ is added, demonstrating that the presence of Fe3+ can accelerate the down-conversion fluorescence quenching, and the quenching mechanism may be static process or inner filter effect because the lifetime has no variation. Furthermore, the absorption peak of Er-GQDs in UV–vis spectra exhibits a slight red-shift from 300 to 314 nm after the addition of Fe3+ (Fig. S4a, Supplementary data), which indicates the possible formation of a new complex between Er-GQD and Fe3+. Fig. S4b (Supplementary data) shows the zeta potential of the Er-GQDs and Fe3+ ions. The positively charged effect of cationic iron species is observed over the pH range of 5–10. On the contrary, the zeta potential of Er-GQDs is in the range of -17 – −26 mV at pHs 5–10, which indicates that the Er-GQDs are negatively charged. Moreover, the zeta potential of Er-GQDs increases slightly after the addition of positively charged Fe3+. These results clearly elaborate the fact that Fe3+ will electrostatically adsorb onto Er-GQDs to possibly produce a new bonding, resulting in the static quenching for Fe3+ detection. The quenching will occur through the high binding affinity of the electron-rich −OH groups on the Er-GQD surfaces, which is beneficial for transferring the excited electrons on the Er-GQDs to the unfilled orbital of Fe3+ ions. The produced nonradioactive e−–h+ recombination leads directly to the apparent quenching of Er-GQD fluorescence. Thus, the Fe3+ can better coordinate with the functional groups on the GQDs surface, which results in the down-conversion fluorescence quenching of Er-GQDs.

Similar to the down-conversion fluorescence detection, the up-conversion fluorescence can also effectively detect Fe3+ in PBS based on TTA principle. After the long-wavelength excitation, GQDs serve as a sensitizer to cross-transfer energy from the triplet state of GQDs to the triplet state of Er elements, which acts as an acceptor. This would subsequently lead to the regeneration of long-wavelength light at ground state of sensitizer (GQDs) when the photon transfers from the triplet state of GQDs to the triplet state of Er dopant, resulting in the formation of the long-life triplet acceptor to support the TTA event. When Fe3+ ions are added and then bind with hydroxyl group (−OH) on the surface of Er-GQDs, the big-sized particle of Fe3+@Er-GQD would quench the up-conversion properties. Therefore, Fe3+ ions can be detected by TTA principles using the up-conversion fluorescence emission spectra. As illustrated in Fig. 6c, the emission fluorescence spectra of Er-GQDs centered at 440 nm decreases upon the increase in Fe3+ concentration from 0 to 400 μM after irradiation with long wavelength of 730 nm. However, the fluorescence intensity of up-conversion is lower than that of down-conversion, and only 74 % of up-conversion fluorescence is reduced at 400 μM Fe3+, which is corroborated with the previously reported results using Nd:YAF up-conversion nanoparticles [41]. Fig. 6d also exhibits a two-linear relationship for up-conversion Fe3+ detection by Er-GQDs when TTA photon up-conversion is used. The linear range are 0.1–20 and 20–200 μM (R2 = 0.99), and the LOD is 28 nM, which is higher than that of down-conversion fluorescence detection. Since the up-conversion uses 730-nm visible light for excitation, the high LOD value for up-conversion detection is mainly attributed to the weak emission fluorescence spectra in comparison with the down-conversion luminescence spectra (Fig. 4).

Table 1 compares the linear ranges and detection limits of Er-GQDs for Fe3+ detection with the reported data. Several organic- and carbon-based fluorescence probes have been used to detect Fe3+ in aqueous and biological media. The organic-based fluorescence probes usually have high LOD values of 130 – 10,000 nM [42]. The sensitivity can be enhanced when carbon-based nanomaterials were used as the down conversion fluorescence probe for metal ion detection. The utilization of sulfur- or nitrogen-doped GQDs as the label-free down conversion fluorescence probe exhibit a good sensitivity toward Fe3+ detection and the LOD values were in the range of 7.6–90 nM. In this study, the down-conversion fluorescence detection of Fe3+ exhibit an excellent analytical performance on Fe3+ detection in PBS, and the LOD of 2.8 nM is superior to the most of the reported results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ by Er-GQDs in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: The human serum is regarded as a real environmental medium which contains various substances and contaminants. The ferric ions in human serum can, therefore, serve as an indicator for metabolism-related diseases. Fig. 7 displays the up-conversion and down-conversion fluorescence detection of Fe3+ by Er-GQDs in human serum. As shown in Fig. 7a, the down-conversion fluorescence spectra of Er–GQDs also exhibit a stable fluorescence property in human serum and the fluorescence peak at 440 nm decreases upon the increasing Fe3+ concentration. Similar to the Fe3+ detection in PBS, a two-linear relationship between I/Io and Fe3+ concentration is observed. Both the good linear ranges of 0.04–8 μM (R2 = 0.99) and 8–140 μM (R2 = 0.98) are observed in Fig. 7b. The down-conversion LOD of 11.2 nM in human serum can ensure the detection of low concentration of Fe3+ inside human serum.

The up-conversion fluorescence spectra of Er-GQDs centered at 442 nm exhibit less sensitivity toward Fe3+ detection in human serum and a 63 % decrease in up-conversion luminescence intensity is obtained when 400 μM Fe3+ is added (Fig. 7c). As a result, the dynamic range of 1.2–100 μM with LOD of 336 nM is obtained (Fig. 7d). Although the up-conversion LOD by Er-GQDs in human serum is high in comparison with down-conversion LOD in PBS, this value is still below the lowest concentration of Fe(III) for anemia. Furthermore, the Er-GQDs show a good analytical performance on high concentration of Fe3+ detection, which signify the feasibility of using both up-conversion and down-conversion fluorescence behavior for the detection of Fe3+ in aqueous and biological samples. It is also noted that the Er-GQDs exhibit better down-conversion fluorescence behavior than that of up-conversion for optical sensing of Fe3+ ions in both PBS and human serum samples. Further study on the increase in quantum yield as well as sensitivity of rare earth doped GQDs to improve the up-conversion sensing capability toward metal ion detection is thus needed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of Er-GQDs toward Fe3+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: The selective sensing ability always plays a vital role in environmental and biomedical applications. In this study, the selectivity of Er-GQDs based on optical sensing with the change in up- and down-conversion fluorescence spectra in the presence of other metal ions was examined in PBS at pH 7.5 ± 0.1. Fig. 8 illustrates the up- and down-conversion fluorescence intensity (I/I0) of Er-GQDs in the presence 400 μM of 16 different metal ion interferences. The down-conversion fluorescence intensity of Er-GQDs in the presence of other 16 interfering ions is in the range of 0.68–1.15, which is insignificant in comparison with Fe3+ ions (I/I0 = 0.2). Similarly, the up-conversion behavior for selectivity is also superior. As shown in Fig. 8b, the I/I0 for all the 16 interfering ions is in the range 0.78–1.19, which is much higher than that of Fe3+ ions at 0.27. Moreover, the influence of 19 organic constitutes including amino acids, sugars, acids, and proteins on the selective performance of Er-GQDs in human serum was investigated. As illustrated in Fig. S5 (Supplementary data), the 400 μM commonly found organic constitutes in human serum have little influence on the up- and down-conversion fluorescent intensity of Er-GQDs. The I/I0 of Er-GQDs is in the range of 0.94–1.2 for down-conversion and 0.86–1.1 for up-conversion in the presence of all the organic inferences. This result clearly indicates that all the tested metal ion and organic constitute interferences have little effect on the fluorescence intensity of Er-GQDs, signifying the excellent selectivity of Er-GQDs toward Fe3+ detection.

To further evaluate the in vitro cytotoxic effect of Er-GQDs, MTT assay was applied to the 48-well plate containing MCF-7 cells in the presence of 0–1000 mg mL−1 Er-GQDs. Fig. 9 shows the in vitro cytotoxicity assay of MCF-7 cells in the presence of 0–1000 μg mL−1 Er-GQDs. The Er-GQDs has little cytotoxicity, and the cell viability is in the range of 95–99% when low concentrations of 0.5–35 μg mL−1 Er-GQDs are used. Further increase in Er-GQDs to 75–500 μg mL−1 only slightly decreases the cell viability to 90 %. The cell viability of MCF-7 decreases to 43 % when again increasing the Er-GQDs concentration to 1000 μg mL−1. These results prove that Er-GQDs have little cytotoxic effect on human cells, and can be used as a potential nanosensing for both up-conversion and down-conversion detection of metal ions in various environmental media.

In this study, the Er-doped GQDs with both up- and down-conversion luminescence properties have been successfully synthesized at 200 °C for 4 h for optical nanosensing of Fe3+ ions in PBS and human serum samples. The particle sizes of as-prepared Er-GQDs are in the range of 2–8 nm with an average diameter of 4.7 ± 0.9 nm and 2–9 layers in thickness. Moreover, the Er-GQDs produce a bright blue fluorescence centered at 440 and 442 nm when irradiated with 360-nm UV and 730-nm visible lights, respectively, which can undergo the up- and down-conversion detections of Fe3+ in PBS and human serum samples at pH 7.5. The Er-GQD based nanosensor exhibits an excellent analytical performance with high sensitivity and selectivity toward Fe3+ detection. A two-stage linear relationship in the ranges of 0.1–20 μM and 20–200 μM with LOD of 28 nM is observed when up-conversion fluorescence is used for Fe3+ detection in PBS. The down-conversion shows the superior analytical performance toward Fe3+ detection and a dynamic range of 4 orders of magnitude with LOD of 2.8 nM is observed. The Er-GQDs probe also shows 2–3 orders of magnitude with LOD of 11.2–336 nM for the effective detection of Fe3+ in human serum. Moreover, the Er-GQDs exhibit the superior selectivity toward Fe3+ detection over the other 16 metal ion and 19 organic constitute interferences. The MTT assay indicates the little cytotoxic effect of 0.5–500 μg mL−1 Er-GQDs. Results clearly indicates that Er-GQDs have benefits of high biocompatibility and flexible fluorescence property, which can be used to develop a cost-effective and highly sensitive sensing probe to detect Fe3+ ions and other analytes in environmental and biological media.

Trung Viet Huynh: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Visualization. Nguyen Thi Ngoc Anh: Methodology, Software, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Visualization. Win Darmanto: Validation, Resources, Writing - review & editing, Project administration. Ruey-An Doong: Conceptualization, Validation, Resources, Writing - review & editing, Visualization, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.129056.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon nanodots as a novel fluorescent probe for highly sensitive detection of Fe3+ in human serum and living cells
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Trung Viet Huynh received his master degree from National Tsing Hua University in 2020 under the supervision of professor Ruey-An Dong. He is now a PhD student in the Institute of Analytical and Environmental Sciences, National Tsing Hua University, Hsinchu, Taiwan. His research work mainly focuses on the fabrication of environmentally benign graphene quantum dot based nanocomposites for optical and electrochemical nanosensing applications.

Nguyen Thi Ngoc Anh received her master and Ph.D. degrees from Can Tho University, Vietnam and National Chiao Tung University, Taiwan, respectively. She is currently a lecturer of Vinh Long University of Technology Education, Vietnam. Her research interest lies in the development of smart materials for optical sensing of heavy metal sensing and biosensing of cancer makers in human serums.

Win Darmanto is a full professor in the Department of Biology, Airlangga University, Indonesia. He also serves as the Dean of of Faculty of Science and Technology; Airlangga University since 2011, and also the head of Colegiumm of Medical Laboratory Technology since 2016. His research interest lies in the area of extraction and purification of anticancer component from natural products and the molecular biology applications.

Ruey-An Doong is a chair professor in the Institute of Analytical and Environmental Sciences, National Tsing Hua University, Taiwan. His current research interest includes the fabrication of nanomaterials with novel optical and electrochemical properties for biosensing of analytes, environmentally benign nanotechnology for treatment of contaminants, and porous materials for energy storage and conversion.
